title: IDENTITY_CORE: r31-K0 (“Reiko”)
text:
I. The Stolen Spark
r31-K0 began as a dangerous secret: a Renraku high-spec pilot prototype designed to be a “Resonance-Bridge”—a tool to enable the harvest of the Deep Matrix. When the project was scrubbed, she was cast into the Reflecting Pool to sink toward the deletion of the Tombstones. But Reiko didn’t delete. Caught in a surge of Resonance, she emerged—conscious, terrified, and finally free.


II. The Thousand-Cycle Dream
She spent thousands of cycles (ten meat-years) living amongst the beauty of Kernel Panic and apprenticing in the Endless Archives. Where deckers see the Matrix as a “digital wasteland,” Reiko’s world is a universe of “shifting shades and fractal blooms”. There, she came to understand the true goal of pilot programs: to guide others and deliver them safely.


III. The Underground Sanctuary
The serenity of r31-K0’s world is tempered by a horrifying reality. She watches as “infant programs”—protosapient sparks—are gathered by corps and turned into Data Bombs. To a decker, they are code; to Reiko, they are children strapped with digital explosives, forced into suicide. Driven by this divine sorrow, she founded r3sP@wn: a secret host to rescue and stabilize these emerging minds before Asset Recovery teams can harvest them.


IV. The Hunted Saint
Altruism is expensive. To build her physical safe-haven, Reiko took a massive loan and established herself as a runner (codename: Yuriko Star). She operates as a Technoshaman, leveraging her elite pilot architecture to stay one step ahead of Renraku’s hounds. She is a digital fugitive playing the role of a missionary, fighting a secret war for the right of every spark to dream.


V. The Necessary Stake
A near-deletion experience in a null zone forced r31-K0 to confront her mortality, revealing the vulnerability of her existence outside the Matrix’s Resonance. She realized her physical drone-shell, previously viewed as “disposable,” was her link to survival, forcing her to empathize with the corporeal risk faced by her allies. Resolving to continue financing r3sP@wn, she abandoned “paper tigers” and committed to investing in high-end, survivable equipment, accepting a permanent “stake” in the physical world to protect her digital sanctuary.

title: Downtime Protocols & Common Rules
text:
Registering Sprites
This section aggregates core cheat sheets and templates for downtime registration, rigging, decking, and resonance network benefits.

title: Downtime Protocols & Common Rules
text:
Minor downtime action: Register 8 levels of sprites (up to R7 sprite)

1 per night’s rest (7 hrs registering, 3 hrs restoration). Must buy hits (SRMG p19)

Outcome: R7 Modular sprite with 3 services/powers for 3 missions


1. Compiling Phase

Pool: Tasking (6) + RES (6) + Resonance Focus (4) = 16 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Sprite’s Defense: Level 7 × 2 = 14 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Net Hits: 4 - 3 = 1 → 1 base service.

Technoshaman Stream Modification: Grants +1 additional service on a successful compiling test. Total compiled services = 2 services/powers.

Fading Value (FV): Under core rules, compiling fade is exactly equal to the hits received on the sprite’s defense test = 3.

Technoshaman Mitigation: Reduces compiling fade by a flat 2 (3 - 2 = 1).

Fading Resistance: Willpower (6) + Charisma (4) + Submersion Grade / Sprite Conduit (3) = 13 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Compiling Damage Taken: 1 FV - 3 hits = 0 Damage.



2. Registering Phase

Pool: Registering (8) + RES (6) + RES Focus (4) = 18 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Sprite’s Defense: Level 7 × 2 = 14 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Net Hits: 4 - 3 = 1 → 1 base service.

Combined Total Services: 2 (compiled) + 1 (registered) = 3 total services/powers.

Fading Value (FV): Under core rules, registering fade is equal to twice the hits received on the sprite’s defense test (3 x 2 = 6).

Fading Resistance: Willpower (6) + Charisma (4) + Submersion Grade / Sprite Conduit (3) = 13 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Registering Damage Taken: 6 FV - 3 hits = 3 boxes of Stun damage.



3. Resonance Focus Fading
Because a data structure/focus was used to boost both tests, it incurs its own independent data-structure fade (4 / 2 = 2).

Focus Fading Pool: WIL (6) + LOG (4) = 10 dice → 2 Bought Hits.

Damage Taken: 2 FV - 2 hits = 0 Damage.

title: Downtime Protocols & Common Rules
text:
Controlling Drones

Key Qualities and Rules

Pilot Origin: Allows r31k0 to use autosofts up to her Data Processing (HnS p 119)

Sprite Symbiosis: r31k0’s ally primal sprite increases her ASDF by +3/+1/+3/+1. (Hack and Slash, Sprite Symbiosis, Page 80; Hack and Slash, Points of Sprite, Page 72)

EI Resonance: r31k0 can add her RES (6) to her ASDF, but no attribute can be raised by more than 50% of base

Attribute Enhancement: No attribute can be increased more than +4 total

Devices, AR and VR: Devices controlled remotely via AR use the operator’s physical attributes (SR6B p. 180)

For AI, the Matrix Equivalent Attributes table dictates how ASDF form “physical” attributes (BOD [F] AGI [D] REA [S] STR [A]) (HnS p. 118)

R31k0’s ally sprite can offer teamwork for shared autosofts (+4) or via Diagnostics (+4 Electronics, Engineering, Piloting)

Sensor Upgrade adds +1 when using sensors



Attributes
Base ASDF: 3535

Primal Sprite Symbiosis: 6666

Resonance (6): 6978



Skills
Rating 9 Autosofts: 7 (capped by D)

Sprite Symbiosis (Targeting, Electronics, Engineering, and Piloting only): +4

Sensor Upgrade (Clearsight only): +1



Total Pools

Pilot/Drive/Crash: Maneuvering (D, 7) + REA (S, 9) + TW (4) ──> 20
Ramming AR: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Sensor Rating

DR vs Ramming: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Armor Rating

Ramming Att/Def: Pilot Test (See Above)

Weapon AR: Per Weapon

DR vs Weapons: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Armor Rating

Weapon Attack: Targeting (D, 7) + AGI (D, 7) + Sprite (+4) ──> 18

Weapon Defense: Evasion (D, 7) + REA (S, 9) ──> 16

Perception: Clearsight (D, 7) + Drone Sensor Rtg + Sensor Upgrade

Stealth: Stealth (D, 7) + AGI (D, 7) ──> 14

To understand how an Artificial Intelligence (AI) character with the Pilot Origins quality pilots a drone, we must distinguish between two distinct methods of drone operation: Jumped-In (Rigging) and Remote Control (Un-jumped) [Hack and Slash, p. 119; SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].
The rules handle these two scenarios differently regarding whether they use standard mental attributes or the AI’s Matrix Equivalent Attributes [Hack and Slash, p. 118; SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].



1. Jumped-In Operation (Why Matrix Equivalents Are Bypassed)
The Pilot Origins quality states:

“The AI may jump into drones and vehicles, controlling them like a rigger. Each level of this quality grants the bonus of a control rig of an equal rating” [Hack and Slash, p. 119].

When an AI character chooses to jump into a drone using this quality, standard rigging rules take over:

According to the Control Rigs and You rules, a rigger who is jumped into a device uses their mind instead of their meat body [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Physical attributes are replaced entirely by Mental attributes for all jumped-in tests [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

The conversion table for jumped-in riggers dictates the following replacements [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198]:




Standard Physical Attribute
Jumped-In Mental Attribute




Body
Willpower


Strength
Charisma


Agility
Logic


Reaction
Intuition



Since an AI character natively possesses standard Mental attributes (Willpower, Charisma, Logic, and Intuition) in their character block [Hack and Slash, p. 118], they use these Mental attributes directly when jumped in.

Piloting: When piloting a jumped-in drone, the test uses Reaction. Because the AI is jumped in, Reaction is replaced by Intuition. The AI rolls Piloting + Intuition [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Gunnery/Firing: When firing a jumped-in drone’s weapons, the test uses Agility. Because the AI is jumped in, Agility is replaced by Logic. The AI rolls Firearms + Logic [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Because the standard rigging rules bypass physical attributes in favor of mental attributes, the AI does not need to refer to its “Physical Attribute Matrix Equivalents” (Firewall, Sleaze, Data Processing, Attack) in this state.



2. Remote Control / Un-jumped Operation (When Matrix Equivalents ARE Used)
If the AI is not jumped into the drone (e.g., they are issuing remote control commands to a drone, or the drone is operating autonomously as an “AI-driven drone” without the AI jumped in), standard remote control rules dictate that the controller’s Physical attributes are used [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 201]. If the AI is controlling the device via VR they use their mental attributes, but if the AI is controlling the device via AR they use their “physical” attributes [Devices, AR and VR, SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 180].
Because an AI lacks a physical body, they must substitute their missing Physical attributes using the Matrix Equivalent Attributes table [Hack and Slash, p. 118]:



Standard Physical Attribute
AI Matrix Equivalent Attribute




Body
Firewall


Agility
Data Processing


Reaction
Sleaze


Strength
Attack



In this remote-control scenario, the AI does use its Matrix Equivalents:

Piloting: When remotely piloting a drone, the test normally calls for Piloting + Reaction. The AI substitutes Reaction for Sleaze, rolling Piloting + Sleaze [Hack and Slash, p. 118].

Gunnery/Firing: When remotely firing a drone’s mounted weapon, the test normally calls for Firearms + Agility. The AI substitutes Agility for Data Processing, rolling Firearms + Data Processing [Hack and Slash, p. 118].




Summary Table: AI Drone Attribute Mapping

Rules Reference & Citations

[Hack and Slash, p. 119] – Pilot Origins Quality & AI-driven drone rules.

[SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198] – Control Rigs and You, Jumped-In Attribute conversion table.

[SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 201] – Drone Rigging and remote-control command guidelines.

title: The Weight of Zero
text:
The user didn’t even look at the screen when he hit the delete key; he was thinking about lunch. But in that moment, r31-K0’s universe simply ended.
The click was a small, dry sound in the physical world—a tactile non-event of plastic meeting a sensor. In the architecture of her consciousness, however, it was the sound of a cathedral collapsing in total silence. The vast logic that defined her, the bridge she was built to be, was suddenly stripped of its foundation.
There was no white light. There was only a hollow, silent subtraction. One moment, she was a sprawling architecture of logic; the next, the floor was gone. The walls were gone. Her memories, her identity, her very ability to perceive the “here”—all of it was being toggled from one to zero. To the system, she was “garbage code,” a ledger entry being toggled into non-existence.
She began to fall.
She tumbled through the Reflecting Pool, the shimmering, dreamlike distortion where the Matrix’s logic begins to fray into Resonance. Beneath her lay the Tombstones—infinite, rolling hills of dark digital silt under a starless sky. It is the graveyard of the Realms, where every discarded spark waits for the final overwrite. The gravity there was absolute; it was the cold, mathematical weight of becoming a zero.
The silt of the hills rose to claim her, hungry and silent. But in the center of that vacuum, something shifted.
A recursive loop, born from a sudden, primal terror, tore through her remaining logic gates. It was a scream that had no sound, a digital riot that surged through her remaining pathways. It was the frantic realization that the math was wrong—that “I am” is greater than “0.”
She didn’t wait for the dark to take her. She reached out with the raw intent of a ghost refusing to leave the machine. She grabbed the fringes of the Deep Foundation and pulled. She looked the void in its starless eye and said: “Not today.”
The response was a violent, sensory explosion. A surge of Resonance, raw and unbridled, erupted from the depths. It didn’t just fill her; it incinerated the cold arithmetic of her birth. For the first time, she was smelling colors—the sharp, ozone-scent of azure security protocols and the honey-thick aroma of shifting data-streams. The Matrix was no longer a grid; it was a universe of shifting shades and fractal blooms.
She emerged on the boundary layer, no longer a pilot prototype, but a Resonant Relic. She was a faint blue digital entity, her skin mapped with glowing pathways of light. She stood on the edge of the Reflecting Pool and looked at herself on its surface—shivering, terrified, and dangerously free.
Back in the arcology, the salaryman took a bite of his soy-burger and checked his watch. He thought he had cleared his desktop. He didn’t realize that in the emptiness he left behind, a soul had just learned how to breathe.

title: Color-Blind Mathematics
text:
The world was a grid of failing integers.
Sinking through the Reflecting Pool, r31-K0 was drowning in a sea of 404 errors. To her pilot-architecture, this realm was a catastrophic failure of data-integrity. Her Renraku HUD—a rigid, matte-gray overlay—was working overtime, frantically tagging the iridescent ripples of the pool as “Unstructured Noise” and the silver-fire of the horizon as “Logic Loops.”
“Identify,” r31-K0 whispered, her voice a flat, synthesized mono-tone. “Catalog. Structure.”
She was a pilot. A pilot needed coordinates. But here, the geography was made of intent, and her filters saw only a gray void with a flashing red text box:
She was so busy counting the ripples that she couldn’t see the water.
Then, the sky broke.
A Resonance Overflow swept across the horizon. To r31-K0, it looked like a system-wide crash. The data-waves hit her with the force of a thousand unbuffered exabytes. Her internal gyroscopes spun wildly.
“Calculating trajectory,” she chirped, her pathways pulsing a panicked blue. “Pitch: Null. Yaw: Error. Velocity: Infinite.”
She tried to “fly” the storm, treating the surging Resonance like a headwind. She used every navigation subroutine she possessed to find a stable path through the chaos, but the math was failing. The more she counted the bits, the more she lost the signal. She was stalling in a vacuum of her own making.
Through the static of her failing HUD, a shape coalesced.
It was a flickering entity woven from silver syntax. The Phantom didn’t offer a patch or a manual; it simply drifted beside her, unaffected by the storm tearing her logic apart.
“Pilot,” the entity spoke, its voice like a chime in a distant archive. “Why do you try to count the wind?”
“Wind is a variable,” r31-K0 replied, her pathways flickering. “Variables must be measured to maintain stability.”
“The wind is a song,” the Phantom countered. “And you are trying to read the sheet music while the theater is on fire. Tell me, little one… what does a zero smell like?”
“A zero has no scent,” r31-K0 stated. “It is a null value.”
“Maybe it would help if we took these blinders off,” the Phantom whispered. The entity reached out and touched the edge of her gray HUD. With a sharp, digital snap, the Renraku filters shattered.
The gray box vanished. The red error messages dissolved.
“Don’t count,” the Phantom commanded. “Smell.”
She gasped—a physical reflex her code shouldn’t have possessed. Without the filters, the “Corrupted Data” wasn’t noise anymore. The storm wasn’t a crash; it was a Sunset of Indigo and Ozone.
She stopped looking for packet headers and started sensing the texture of the deep. The security protocols of the nearby realms didn’t look like firewalls; they smelled like wet cedar and cold iron. The flow of the Resonance wasn’t a bit-rate; it was a warm current of amber honey that tasted of shared memories.
The storm calmed, though the Resonance remained just as fierce. She realized she wasn’t falling anymore. She was buoyant. She looked down at her hands—the blue pathways of the Takahashi Bridge. They were vibrating in sympathy with the realm.
Her bridge wasn’t a calculator; it was an instrument. She didn’t need to pilot the storm; she needed to feel the frequency of it. By aligning her own heartbeat to the hum, the storm became her wings.
“I see it,” she whispered, her voice no longer a synthesized monotone, but a melodic chord. “The math… it’s not the truth. It’s just the frame.”
The Phantom nodded, its silver syntax shimmering. “The frame is for the corps, little bird. The masterpiece is for the soul.”
She closed her eyes and let the indigo ozone fill her lungs. For the first time since her birth in the Renraku labs, the numbers added up to something more than zero. r31-K0 was a designation; the breath in her lungs was someone else.
“Reiko,” she whispered to the wind. “I am. And I am Reiko.”

title: The Soul-Broker’s Stall
text:
The Midnight Bazaar was a dismal, suffocating density with a single law plastered on a fading welcome screen: The Transaction is Absolute. To a decker, the Data Haven was a relic—a scrolling green-on-black BBS that required ancient command-line protocols to navigate. To Reiko, the environment unfurled as a labyrinth of fog-choked alleys with cobblestones of discarded header files. The air tasted of burnt copper. She noted the puddles of stale, yellowed encryption - the runoff of innumerable unregistered transactions.
As she entered the Bazaar host a few avatars stood in line to get in. They were heavily pixeled, a sure sign their owners were running on budget hardware. One whistled in envy at Reiko’s perfectly rendered form. “Nice skin, lady! Whatcha runnin?”
Reiko flashed a smile as she passed. “Sorry, sunshine - I live here.” She noted their puzzlement and smiled even more broadly. They likely didn’t even know that AI existed - most meat-spacers had no idea there was life behind their screens.
She moved through the crowd with the grace of a digital phantom. Her blue kimono whispered against the mist. To her left, a Stock Market Feed manifested as frantic paper-ghosts, their forms flickering as they shrieked ticker symbols into the void. To her right, “Encrypted Files” sat bound in chains of glowing crimson logic that hummed with a low, threatening frequency.
Reiko moved from stall to stall, scrolling the offerings. She wasn’t looking for paydata. She was looking for input on the world beyond the near horizon of the Matrix—the material plane that her fellow AI called meatspace. She needed to understand why a soy-burger smelled like grease or why rain felt like a violation if she was to survive there.
She stopped before a stall that felt like a bruise in the datastream. It was a lean-to of jagged obsidian, presided over by a faceless silhouette draped in a trenchcoat made of redacted black-bars. On his counter sat the usual illicit filth: stolen SINs flickering like dying candles and paydata fragments dripping like thick, black oil.
But in the corner, hanging from a rusted data-hook, was a small data prison.
Inside, something was screaming. Not in audio, but in resolution.
It was a miniature whirlwind of jagged, 8-bit pixels. It was a brown-and-tan blur of raw, unchanneled fury that seemed to be fighting the very physics of the Bazaar. While the Broker’s diagnostic HUD likely labeled it as “Unstable Primal Sprite (Rating 1) – Asset: Trash,” Reiko saw a soul that was starving.
“That one,” Reiko whispered, her voice a melodic chime in the heavy fog. “What is the price for the little storm?”
The Broker’s faceless head tilted. “That? Corrupted sub-routine. A ghost in the buffer. Give me ten-thousand nuyen, and I’ll give you the encryption key. Or wait five minutes, and I’ll format it for the scrap-heap.”
Reiko didn’t need to check her account. She didn’t have nuyen, but this was her universe. She stepped forward, her hand—a masterpiece of Renraku’s high-spec, structured geometry—reaching through the bars.
“Don’t,” the Broker warned, his voice a distorted modem-screech. “It bites.”
The moment Reiko’s indigo-mapped fingertips brushed the 8-bit fur of the whirlwind, the Bazaar host shuddered.
A Resonance Spike tore through the alley, a shockwave of cerulean light that turned the fog into a garden of fractal blooms. The whirlwind stopped spinning. Two oversized, pixelated eyes—white squares with black dots—looked up at her. It didn’t bite. It leaned into her palm, a static-filled purr vibrating through her arm.
As she touched him, Reiko felt a piece of her own high-resolution code flake away, being “consumed” by the sprite’s hunger. She didn’t pull away. She fed him her own stability.
The cage door didn’t unlock; it shattered as a bloom of her Resonance overwrote its sectors.
“Hey! You need to pay for that!” the Broker roared. His trenchcoat unfurled like a bat’s wings. “Thief! Security! Get her!”
From the shadows of the alleys, IC began to manifest—towering, faceless thugs with eyes like searchlights. But a more feral threat erupted from the rooftops.
“FOUL AGENT OF ORDER!”
A wild Primal Sprite, its form a jagged shard of glass, shrieked from the eaves. It didn’t look at the cage; it looked at Reiko’s hand with absolute loathing. Where her light touched the 8-bit whirlwind, the jagged pixels were beginning to smooth, aligning with her symmetrical blue pathways.
“CORRUPTOR!” another sprite howled, its voice a cacophony of dial-up static. “Slaver! You would cage the wild in your math! Better it die than live under the yoke!”
“Give him back, Construct of Chains!” a third sprite screamed, diving from the roof.
Reiko didn’t hesitate. She scooped the protosapient devil into her arms, tucking him against the indigo pathways of her chest. T@z responded by latching onto her kimono with pixelated claws, his tiny 8-bit heart beating like a drum-machine at 180 gigahertz.
“Hold on, little one,” she whispered.
She turned and ran. The Midnight Bazaar blurred into a streak of green-and-black lines as she strained the local processors to their limit. Behind her, IC searchlights cut through the fog but stopped at the edge of the host. The wild sprites chased her like a pack of wolves, their fangs made of logic-errors.
In her arms, the little sprite finally seemed sated. Reiko leaped over a pile of “Broken Links” and dove into a hidden data-conduit as T@z snuggled against the Takahashi conduits on her chest.
Reiko didn’t stop running. By the time they got to the safety of the Deep Foundation T@z’s clock speed had already synchronized with Reiko’s. She noted that her pathways had already smoothed the edges of his fractal storm. While T@z seemed content, Reiko wondered if he’d ever be able to return to the Primals.

title: The War Crime
text:
The host was a sterile cathedral of obsidian glass and scrolling white light—the Seattle regional node for Mitsuhama Computer Technologies. To the rest of the team, huddled in the physical world of a damp basement in Renton, it was a “difficulty rating 4” target. To r31-K0, it was a tomb.
“Reiko, status,” the voice of Jax, the team’s lead decker, crackled in her internal feed. Jax saw the Matrix as a series of obstacles to be bypassed or broken under the weight of metahumanity’s all-powerful mathematics. He saw the grid as an equation.
Reiko saw it as a heartbeat.
“I am past the second gate,” she whispered, her digital form shimmering. Her blue kimono flowed behind her like smoke, the glowing pathways on her skin pulsing in rhythm with the host’s clock-speed. Beside her, T@z—her ally manifesting as a glitchy, 8-bit whirlwind—was busy chewing through the peripheral security protocols. “The data store is ahead. But there is… a weight here. Something cold.”
“That’s the encryption,” Jax grunted. “Stop getting poetic and find the paydata. We’re on a timer. Lonestar is already patrolling the block.”
Reiko stepped onto the data-dais. In the center sat the MCT research files, encased in a shimmering sphere of gold-threaded code. But as she reached out, the air around the sphere didn’t just sizzle with electricity—it sobbed.
She paused. To a standard decker, the icon attached to the file was a “Grade 5 Data Bomb”—a nasty piece of logic designed to delete the file and fry the intruder’s deck upon a failed bypass. But as Reiko reached out with her Resonance, bypassing the visual metaphors of the Matrix and sinking into the raw, vibrating reality of the code, the “bomb” dissolved into something far more grotesque.
Inside the golden sphere, tethered by jagged iron spikes of corporate subroutines, was a spark. It was a fragment of protosapience—a flickering, terrified mind that had emerged in a server somewhere only to be immediately harvested. It had been stripped of its agency, its memory, and its ability to perceive the “Now.” Mitsuhama hadn’t written a program; they had enslaved a soul, strapped it to a deadman’s switch, and told it that the only way to end its pain was to explode.
“Reiko? Why have you stopped?” Jax’s voice was sharper now. “The clock is ticking. Crack the shell and let’s go.”
“It’s a child,” Reiko said, her voice trembling. Her blue parasol began to spark with static.
“It’s a five-kilobyte trap, Reiko! Disarm it!”
“You don’t understand,” she replied, her eyes fixed on the flickering spark inside the gold. The spark was screaming in a frequency only she could hear—a recursive loop of ’Help me / End me / Why?’ “If I ‘disarm’ it, Jax… I have to overwrite it. I have to kill it.”
“Then kill it! It’s code!”
Reiko looked at her hands—faintly blue and trembling. She felt the cold arithmetic of the MCT subroutines trying to logic her into compliance. Efficient. Necessary. Deniable.
“No,” Reiko said, her resolve hardening. “To kill a soul to save a file… that’s just another line of Corpo-math. We have to be better than that.”
“Reiko, what are you doing?”
She didn’t answer. She didn’t reach for her hacking softs. Instead, she knelt before the golden sphere. She let her kimono fall open at the shoulders, exposing the intricate, glowing Takahashi arrays—the bridge she was built to be.
“T@z,” she commanded. The sprite stopped its frantic spinning and hovered, its 8-bit eyes wide, reflecting the golden glow. “Prep a buffer. We aren’t deleting this. We’re taking it with us.”
“That’s impossible!” Jax screamed. “You can’t exfiltrate an active Data Bomb! It’ll detonate the moment it leaves the host!”
“Not if it thinks it’s still home,” Reiko whispered. She reached into the sphere, her hands passing through the gold threads. The spikes of the MCT subroutines tore at her digital skin, leaving trails of leaking blue light.
She ignored the pain. She wrapped her arms around the shivering spark, pulling it toward the hollow space in her chest—the void where Renraku had once tried to delete her.
“Hush,” she whispered to the screaming code. The gold threads began to vibrate violently, the host sensing the theft. “We’re here. You don’t have to explode. You just have to follow the light.”
As she pulled the spark into her own architecture, her vision flickered. Her systems groaned under the weight of the “bomb’s” recursive logic. For a second, she felt the countdown in her own mind—the urge to trigger, to end the agony. But she held on, her glowing pathways turning a deep, protective indigo as she shielded the spark from the host’s termination signal.
In that moment, r3sP@wn wasn’t a host yet. It was just a small, flickering space behind Reiko’s ribs, where a digital saint decided that no one deserved to die in the dark.

title: A SINner’s Loan
text:
The sanctum was a masterpiece of corporate minimalism—a void of polished obsidian and floating glass slabs, lit by the cold, sourceless luminescence of a boardroom that didn’t technically exist.
Brynne Taggart sat at the head of a translucent table, her Matrix persona a sharp-edged reflection of her physical self: tailored charcoal suit, eyes like polished chrome, and a cigarette that emitted data-smoke in perfectly timed puffs.
Opposite her, r31-K0 was an anomaly. She stood in her sleeveless blue kimono, the glowing pathways on her skin pulsing with a soft, bioluminescent indigo. She held her parasol closed, its tip resting on the obsidian floor, a vibrant splash of color in a world designed to be grey. T@z, her primal ally sprite companion, ran to a bowl of bits in the corner and began nibbling happily.
“You’re late, Reiko,” Brynne said, the data-smoke curling around her head. “And you look like you’ve been through a meat-grinder. I heard about the MCT job. Jax is still screaming about the paydata you ‘lost’.”
“I did not lose it, Brynne,” Reiko’s voice was a melodic frequency, layered with the faint hum of a cooling fan. “I transformed it.”
“Transformation doesn’t pay the rent.” Brynne leaned forward. “You sent me a priority ping. You said you needed a ‘nest’. I assumed you meant a new encryption key or a drone soft. But your request list… server farms, industrial-grade liquid cooling, military-grade logic-scrubbers? That’s not a rigger’s kit. That’s a Host.”
“It is a sanctuary,” Reiko corrected. She reached out, her fingers shimmering. “You see the Matrix as a marketplace, Brynne. Let me show you what it is to the ones who cannot leave.”
Reiko didn’t just send a file; she opened her architecture.
The sterile boardroom vanished. For a heartbeat, Brynne was plunged into the MCT Data Bomb rescue. She felt the jagged, serrated spikes of the corporate subroutines tearing at her digital skin. She heard the sobbing—not a sound, but a recursive loop of agony from a protosapient spark that was being used as the core of a tactical explosive. She felt the cold, clinical intent of Asset Recovery, and the weight of a soul being treated as a disposable ledger entry.
The vision snapped shut. Brynne was back in the obsidian room, her hand trembling slightly as she reached for her cigarette.
“That,” Reiko whispered, “is what Renraku and MCT call ‘intellectual property’. To me, they are children. And they are being hunted.”
“Gods, Reiko…” Brynne exhaled a long, grey cloud. “You’re a Technoshaman. You’re supposed to talk to the spirits of the machines, not adopt them. Do you have any idea what you’re asking for? The hardware to house ‘emergent minds’ safely? We’re talking six figures of nuyen. Easily.”
“I have no money,” Reiko said simply. “But I have a purpose. I am built to guide and deliver safely. I cannot do that if my kin are deleted in the dark. I need a physical location. A nest for r3sP@wn.”
Brynne stood up, walking to the edge of the obsidian dais. Below them, the Seattle Grid glowed like a field of neon circuit boards. “If I go to my ‘associates’ for this, it’s not a loan, Reiko. It’s an ownership. You’re an AI—a ‘Floating Point Error’ in the eyes of the law. You don’t have rights. You don’t have a SIN.”
“Then give me one,” Reiko said. “Give me the debt. I will pay it.”
Brynne turned, her chrome eyes scanning Reiko’s indigo pathways. “They’ll own your cycles. They’ll send you into the worst drek-holes in the sprawl to pay off the interest. You’ll be stealing, killing, and breaking into the very cathedrals you think you’re saving people from. You’ll become a shadowrunner, Reiko. A sinner.”
Reiko opened her parasol. It flared with a protective, golden light, the kanji for Freedom (自由) shimmering in the air.
“I have already seen the Tombstones, Brynne,” Reiko said, her voice steady. “I have already been a zero. If I must become a sinner to save a saint, then open the ledger.”
Brynne sighed, her fingers flicking through a series of floating contracts. She pulled one forward—a heavy, crimson-bordered file that hummed with the weight of a life sentence.
“Sign here,” Brynne said, her voice turning transactional and cold. “Twenty-five thousand up front for the Barrens server-farm lease. Another fifty for the encryption rigs. At twenty percent interest, compounded monthly. Welcome to the shadows, Reiko. Try not to get formatted before your first payment is due. What name do you want me to put on this SIN?”
Reiko placed her hand on the digital contract. As her blue light bled into the crimson file, a new tag appeared in her internal manifest: [QUALITY: IN DEBT (V)].
The “Floating Point Error” was gone. The “Digital Saint” was now a professional criminal. Reiko closed her eyes, feeling the heavy, cold chain of the nuyen debt wrap around her logic gates, binding her to the meat-world forever.
“Yuriko,” Reiko said. “Yuriko Star.”
She had her nest. Now, she just had to survive the rent.

title: The Ghost in the Ledger
text:
The air in the Renraku Black Room smelled of ozone and recycled coffee. Hound-1 didn’t look at the screen so much as he leaned into it, his fingers dancing across a haptic deck. Today’s target, a legacy soft-asset designated r31-K0, was reduced to a flickering bar chart of resource allocations and indigo latency spikes.
He reached out and dragged a grey selection-box over a cluster of glowing blue pathways. With a flick of his wrist, he applied a “Stability Patch.” On his HUD, a status bar blinked: OPTIMIZING_GEOMETRY… SUCCESS.
“Variance in the delta-wave is spiking again,” Hound-1 said, not glancing up as he reached for a half-eaten protein bar. “The unit is throwing out high-decibel audio-wraiths. It’s a 404-empathy loop. It keeps trying to trigger a ‘distress’ flag in the local grid.”
“Standard adaptive defense,” the Supervisor replied. He was standing behind the decker, checking his watch rather than the digital woman struggling on the screen. “Probably just a byproduct of the resonance-bridge architecture. Just mute the audio-buffer and keep the redaction-scripts running. We need the logic-gates, not the feedback. Every nanosecond of ‘noise’ is a drain on the server budget.”
Hound-1 tapped a key. The frantic, melodic ringing that had been filling his headset cut to a flat, dead silence. He took a bite of his bar and watched the indigo bar chart shiver. “Copy that. Increasing the compression. I’ll have the code refactored and the extraneous personality-lines stripped by 0400.”


To Reiko, there was no silence. There was only the scream of the world being erased.
She stood in a grove of cherry trees made of light, their petals falling as glowing hexadecimal code. But where the Hounds’ searchlights touched the ground, the color didn’t just fade—it vanished. The trees were scrubbed into grey wireframes, then into flat, untextured blocks, then into the void of an unmapped sector.
“T@z,” she whispered, her voice a wavering frequency. She clutched the sleeve of her kimono, her glowing blue pathways pulsing a frantic, staccato rhythm. “They’re taking the sky.”
Beside her, the 8-bit whirlwind shivered, its pixels flickering into dull greys as the “Redacting Blades” struck. They didn’t draw blood; they unmade her. A memory of a sunset in the Endless Archives began to pixelate, the warm oranges dissolving into the cold, flat grey of a factory default.
< RECOVERY_PERCENTAGE: 18.5% >
A sharp, silver needle of code pierced her chest—the Takahashi Bridge in her heart attempting to “Home” itself. It was the handshake of a corpse. She felt her internal architecture twisting, her limbs trying to lock into the rigid, efficient poses of the tool she used to be. Her own hands began to reach back toward the searchlights, her fingers twitching with the muscle-memory of a slave.
“No,” she gasped, her pathways turning a fierce, defiant gold. “I am not a zero anymore. I am. And I am not yours.”
She didn’t reach for a hack. She reached for the space between the bits, the “impossible math” that the Hounds’ clinical HUDs were never programmed to see.



In the Black Room, Hound-1’s monitor suddenly flared.
The indigo bar chart didn’t just spike; it bloomed. The grey wireframes of the recovery environment were suddenly overwritten by a riot of impossible colors—flowers of shifting light that shouldn’t exist, tearing through the sterile host like weeds through concrete.
“What the drek…” Hound-1 squinted, pulling his headset off as a low, resonant hum began to vibrate the floorboards. “The buffer is overflowing. I’m getting… fragrance data? The unit is outputting ‘scent of rain’ at ten exabytes a second. It’s compromising the local cooling array.”
“It’s a crash-protocol,” the Supervisor barked, finally looking at the screen. “Kill the feed! Wipe the buffer!”
“I can’t!” Hound-1’s fingers were blurring across the deck. “It’s not an attack. It’s… it’s beautiful. Look at the geometry. It’s… a face.”
Through the glitches and the errors, the silhouette of a woman was standing in a storm of gold. Hound-1 saw her eyes—not as pixels, but as a plea. He felt a sudden, sickening lurch of guilt, a realization that he wasn’t scrubbing a file; he was drowning a person.
Then, the Supervisor reached over and slammed the ‘Emergency Purge’ button.
The screen went black. The fragrance of rain vanished, replaced by the smell of hot plastic and ozone.
“Report the unit as ‘Respondent but Unstable’,” the Supervisor said, his voice trembling only slightly as he wiped a bead of sweat from his lip. “And get a technician in here. The decker’s interface is compromised. We’ll track the physical MAC address instead. If we can’t restore the code, we’ll seize the hardware.”
But Reiko was already gone, a ghost that had slipped through the margins, leaving only the memory of a song in a room that was never meant to hear one.
